fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the core backend for our new analytics data collection pipeline. Basically, we're getting hammered with support tickets because our current setup loses visitor identity between page loads, and the ops team can't configure anything without a code deploy. Super frustrating.

The big ask: we need something that tracks visitors with stable IDs that survive cookie round-trips, reads config values safely without blowing up when something's missing (you know how ops always forgets to set every field), maps incoming web hits into clean typed records using a config file instead of hardcoded logic, and writes everything out to files in a way that downstream can trust — i.e., only fully-written files should look 'done'.

For the file writing part, we need two modes: one that just rolls files on a schedule, and one that groups events by 'which time window did this session start in' — similar to how we handled the batching logic in that old session affinity module. If a session started in an earlier window and that file is still open, the event should go there.

One thing I keep forgetting to mention: the config mapping layer needs to loudly reject bad setups early — operators should know immediately if something's misconfigured, not find out hours later from missing data. Please keep all output as plain key=value lines, one per line.

trigger_keywords: how long is the encoded token
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The identity token must encode to exactly 32 characters (fixed width), regardless of the timestamp value. The token embeds a millisecond timestamp plus random bits, encoded into a hex-like opaque string.

A token decoded/re-parsed from its string representation must equal the original token (value equality and same hashcode). However, an independently generated token for the same timestamp must NOT be equal, because the random bits will differ.

trigger_keywords: what happens when config path is missing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a config path is absent, the accessor must return an 'absent' state rather than throwing an exception. The output should be 'state=absent\n' with no value line. It must never crash on a missing path.

When a config value is present, return it as-is. When absent, yield the supplied fallback value (both eager and lazy resolution are supported). Output is 'value=<resolved>\n' in both cases — no state line, just the value.

When a required config path is absent, the system must surface a neutral error code 'absent_value'. Output should be 'error=absent_value\n'. It must not throw an exception — it surfaces a categorized neutral error.

A present list that satisfies the predicate (i.e., contains the required element) stays present and outputs state=present, size=<N>, items=<comma-separated>. A list that fails the predicate OR an absent path both become absent (state=absent\n only).

The config map/transform operation applies a function (e.g., string length) to a present value and returns the transformed result as 'state=present
value=<transformed>
'. If the path is absent, the function is never called and output is 'state=absent
'.

A mapping configuration with an unsupported version must be rejected at construction time (fail-fast). The output must be 'error=unsupported_mapping_version\nversion=<N>\n' where N is the unsupported version number.

A mapping that references a field not present in the output schema must be rejected at construction time. The output must be 'error=missing_schema_field\nfield=<fieldName>\n' where fieldName is the name of the unrecognized field.

When a string value from a query param, URL path, or cookie cannot be parsed into the schema's required type (int, long, double, boolean), the field falls back to the schema's declared default for that field. For example, if pathInteger cannot be parsed, it outputs the schema default (e.g., -1).

An in-flight (not yet finalized) file must use a '.avro.partial' suffix and must be reported as '<unreadable>' in the output — it is not a valid durable container until finalized. A published file uses the '.avro' suffix and shows its record timestamps as comma-separated values.

The simple/rolling strategy writes all records to a single in-flight file, publishing it on clean shutdown. If rolls are forced between batches, each interval produces one published file. Output lines are: published_files=N, inflight_files=N, then published[i]=.avro|<ts-list> and inflight[i]=.avro.partial|<unreadable> in order.

In the binning strategy, each event is routed to the file for the window in which its session started (based on session_start timestamp). If that window's file is still open, the event goes there. If the window is no longer current, the event goes to the current window's file. On shutdown, in-flight binning files are finalized in place but NOT published — they remain as .avro.partial.

The sessionStart field is a derived boolean indicating whether this event is the first event in the session. It is true when the session is new (e.g., page_view_id matches the session start condition). Output as 'sessionStart=true' or 'sessionStart=false'.

When no geo lookup result is available, all geo fields must be null (output as 'null'). When a geo lookup returns an empty/default result, boolean geo fields output false and list fields output empty lists. When a full result is available, all fields are populated with actual values.

All output must be plain 'key=value' lines, one per line, in a fixed order. Collections render as '[a, b, c]' (space after comma). Absent/null values render as 'null'. No JSON, no headers, no extra whitespace beyond what is specified.

A cookie mapping that omits its required cookie name, or an event-parameter mapping that omits its required parameter name, must be rejected at mapper construction time with a descriptive categorized error. This is part of fail-fast validation.

For regex named-mapping, a field is populated by the named capture group of a regular expression applied to a request signal (such as location or referer URL). For example, a regex applied to 'http://example.com/' with a named group 'protocol' would yield 'http'.

The user-agent string is parsed into: userAgentName, userAgentFamily, userAgentVendor, userAgentType, userAgentVersion, userAgentDeviceCategory, userAgentOsFamily, userAgentOsVersion, userAgentOsVendor. These are populated from the raw User-Agent header using a UA parsing library.

A large batch of freshly generated tokens (e.g., 100,000) must all be distinct. The random bits embedded in the token ensure uniqueness even for the same timestamp. The uniqueness check output is 'generated=<N>\ndistinct=<N>\n'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'token_encode_decode', 'token_identity', 'token_unique', 'config_presence', 'config_fallback', 'config_required', 'config_filter', 'config_map', 'map_event', 'sink'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled the batching logic in that old session affinity module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The session-binning file sink strategy in Feature 4.2: events are routed to per-session-window files based on when the session started (session_start timestamp determines the window), and an event sticks to the window's file if still open, otherwise moves to the current window's file. In-flight binning files are finalized but NOT published on shutdown.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The identity token codec (Feature 1): a millisecond timestamp plus random bits encoded into a fixed-width 32-character hex-like opaque string with value-based equality, used to label visitors, sessions, and page views. Tokens must survive serialize→cookie→read-back round trips.

Atomic publication semantics: in-flight files use a '.avro.partial' suffix and are reported as '<unreadable>'; only finalized/published files have the '.avro' suffix and are considered durable containers. A file is only readable once finalized.

Fail-fast validation at mapper construction time: unsupported mapping version → error=unsupported_mapping_version; field not in schema → error=missing_schema_field; cookie mapping without name or event-parameter mapping without name → categorized construction-time errors.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect of the requirement. Please check the existing codebase and test cases for the expected behavior — the test cases are the authoritative contract for output format and edge cases. If you're unsure about a specific field or operation, look at the public test cases in the rcb_tests directory for concrete examples.

feature1_1_encode_decode.json.description: Identity token codec: a timestamp encoded into an opaque fixed-width token is recovered intact on decode.

feature1_2_identity.json.description: Token value equality: a decoded token equals its original (same value and hash); an independently generated token for the same timestamp does not.

feature1_3_uniqueness.json.description: Token uniqueness: a large batch of freshly generated tokens are all distinct.

feature2_1_presence.json.description: Optional config lookup: present typed values report present with their value; an absent path reports absent rather than raising.

feature2_2_fallback.json.description: Optional config with fallback: a present value is returned; an absent path yields the supplied fallback, whether resolved eagerly or lazily.

feature2_3_required.json.description: Optional config required: a present value is returned; an absent path surfaces a neutral missing-value error.

feature2_4_filter.json.description: Optional config filtering: a present list that satisfies the predicate stays present; one that fails the predicate or is absent becomes absent.

feature2_5_map.json.description: Optional config transformation: a present value is mapped through a function (here string length); an absent path stays absent without applying the function.

feature3_10_mapping_errors.json.description: Mapping configuration validation: an unsupported mapping version, a mapped field missing from the schema, a cookie mapping without a name, and an event-parameter mapping without a name are each rejected at construction with a descriptive, categorized error.

feature3_1_standard_fields.json.description: Event mapping of standard fields: simple request signals (location, referer, user agent, viewport, event type) and the request's identity tokens are copied into the record, with the derived session-start flag and event timestamp.

cases.expected_output:
sessionStart=true
ts=42
location=https://example.com/
referer=http://example.com/
userAgentString=Divolte/Test
eventType=pageView
viewportWidth=640
viewportHeight=480
client=000000000000002a1111111111111111
session=000000000000002a2222222222222222
pageview=000000000000002a3333333333333333

feature3_2_user_agent.json.description: User-agent parsing: the raw user-agent header is decomposed into browser, vendor, type, version, device category and operating-system attributes.

input.user_agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10_9_4) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/36.0.1985.125 Safari/537.36

cases.expected_output:
userAgentName=Chrome
userAgentFamily=Chrome
userAgentVendor=Google Inc.
userAgentType=Browser
userAgentVersion=36.0.1985.125
userAgentDeviceCategory=Personal computer
userAgentOsFamily=OS X
userAgentOsVersion=10.9.4
userAgentOsVendor=Apple Computer, Inc.

feature3_3_regex_name.json.description: Regex named-mapping: a field is populated by the named capture of a regular expression applied to a request signal (here the URL protocol of location and referer).

feature3_4_regex_group.json.description: Regex capture-group mapping: multiple fields are populated from positional capture groups of regular expressions applied to the location and referer.

feature3_5_query_param.json.description: Query-string parameter mapping: a field is set from a named parameter of the location URL; when the parameter is absent the schema default is used.

feature3_6_type_casting.json.description: Type casting of mapped values: string-typed signals from query, URL path and cookie are cast to the schema's integer, boolean, double and long types; a value that cannot be parsed falls back to the schema default.

cases.expected_output:
queryparamInteger=-42
queryparamBoolean=true
queryparamDouble=3.14159265359
queryparamLong=34359738368
pathInteger=42
pathBoolean=false
pathDouble=3.14159265359
pathLong=34359738368
cookieInteger=42
cookieBoolean=true

feature3_7_custom_cookie.json.description: Custom cookie mapping: a field is populated from the value of a named request cookie.

feature3_8_event_parameter.json.description: Custom event-parameter mapping: a field is populated from a named custom event parameter carried on the request, preserving punctuation in the value.

feature3_9_geoip.json.description: Geo-IP enrichment: when a geo lookup yields a full result every geo field is populated; an empty result yields neutral defaults (false booleans, empty lists, null scalars); no lookup result leaves every geo field null.

feature4_1_rolling.json.description: Rolling file sink: records written under a time-based rolling strategy accumulate in a single in-flight file, published on clean shutdown; an in-flight file that has not been finalized is not yet a readable container; forcing rolls between batches publishes one file per interval.

feature4_2_session_binning.json.description: Session-binning file sink: events are routed to per-session-window files. Events from distinct windows land in separate files; an event whose session opened in an earlier window sticks to that window's file; once a window is no longer current a later event opens the next window's file; a lone event yields a single in-flight file.

inflight[2]=.avro.partial|4100
